r_n.name: radius of nth orbit
r_n.note: r_n = n²a₀ where a₀ = 0.529 Å (Bohr radius)

E_n.name: energy of nth orbit
E_n.note: E_n = -13.6/n² eV; negative indicates bound state

L.name: orbital angular momentum
L.note: L = nℏ = nh/(2π); quantized in integer multiples of ℏ

v_n.name: velocity of electron in nth orbit
v_n.note: v_n = (e²)/(2ε₀nh) = v₁/n; speed decreases with higher orbits

Z.name: atomic number
Z.note: Z=1 for hydrogen; for hydrogen-like ions, E_n = -13.6Z²/n² eV

locked_facts: rₙ = n²a₀ = n² × 0.529 Å — orbital radius grows as the square of quantum number
Eₙ = -13.6/n² eV — energy of nth orbit; ground state (n=1) has E₁ = -13.6 eV
L = nℏ = nh/(2π) — angular momentum is quantized in integer multiples of ℏ = 1.055×10⁻³⁴ J·s
1/λ = R(1/n₁² - 1/n₂²) — Rydberg formula for emission wavelength; R = 1.097×10⁷ m⁻¹
ΔE = E_final - E_initial = 13.6(1/n₁² - 1/n₂²) eV — energy of emitted photon during transition
Lyman series: transitions to n=1 (UV); Balmer series: transitions to n=2 (visible); Paschen: to n=3 (IR)
Ionization energy of hydrogen = 13.6 eV (energy needed to remove electron from ground state to n=∞)
truth_statements: Electrons in Bohr model orbit the nucleus ONLY in specific allowed orbits where angular momentum L = nℏ — orbits between these are forbidden
An electron in a stable orbit does NOT radiate energy, despite undergoing centripetal acceleration — this was Bohr's radical departure from classical electrodynamics
Energy is emitted or absorbed ONLY during transitions between orbits: photon energy equals the energy difference ΔE = |E_final - E_initial|
The Bohr model correctly predicts hydrogen's spectral lines (Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, Brackett, Pfund series) and ionization energy of 13.6 eV
The Bohr model fails for multi-electron atoms because it does not account for electron-electron repulsion or wave-mechanical effects

aha_moment.description: Student sees an electron dropping from n=3 to n=2 and a red photon (656.3 nm — Hα line) being emitted. The calculated energy difference ΔE = 13.6(1/4 - 1/9) = 1.89 eV matches E = hc/λ exactly. The entire visible hydrogen spectrum (red, blue-green, blue, violet) emerges from just 4 transitions to n=2.
aha_moment.visual_cue: Energy level diagram with animated electron jumps producing colored photon arrows; emitted spectrum matches laboratory hydrogen discharge tube spectrum

STATE_1.title: Allowed Orbits and Energy Levels
STATE_1.description: Show concentric circular orbits for n=1 through n=5 around a proton nucleus. Label each orbit with its radius (rₙ = n²×0.529 Å) and energy (Eₙ = -13.6/n² eV). Display the energy level diagram alongside. Electron sits in ground state n=1.

STATE_2.title: Photon Absorption — Excitation
STATE_2.description: An incoming photon with exactly ΔE = 10.2 eV (Lyman-α) hits the atom. Electron absorbs it and jumps from n=1 to n=2. Show that a photon with slightly wrong energy is NOT absorbed (resonance condition). Display ΔE = 13.6(1/1² - 1/2²) = 10.2 eV.

STATE_3.title: Emission Transitions — Spectral Series
STATE_3.description: Electron in n=3 drops to n=2 emitting Hα (red, 656.3 nm), then show n=4→2 (Hβ, blue-green, 486.1 nm), n=5→2 (Hγ, blue, 434.0 nm), n=6→2 (Hδ, violet, 410.2 nm). Build up the Balmer series visually. Show Lyman (UV) and Paschen (IR) series too.

STATE_4.title: Interactive Exploration
STATE_4.description: Student selects initial and final orbits. Simulation calculates ΔE, wavelength, and identifies the spectral series. Electron animates the transition and emitted photon color is shown. Challenge: which transition gives the shortest wavelength in Balmer series?

common_misconceptions.misconception: Orbiting electrons should continuously radiate energy and spiral into the nucleus according to classical physics
common_misconceptions.correct: Bohr POSTULATED that electrons in allowed orbits do NOT radiate. This was a deliberate break from classical electrodynamics, justified by the stability of atoms and later explained by quantum mechanics (standing wave condition).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Electrons in higher orbits move faster because they have more energy
common_misconceptions.correct: Electrons in HIGHER orbits move SLOWER (vₙ = v₁/n). The higher total energy (less negative) is because potential energy increases more than kinetic energy decreases. E_n is less negative but KE is actually smaller.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Any photon with energy greater than the transition energy can be absorbed by the atom
common_misconceptions.correct: For bound-bound transitions, the photon energy must EXACTLY match the energy difference between two levels. A 11 eV photon cannot cause the n=1→n=2 transition (which requires exactly 10.2 eV). Only ionization (n→∞) has a continuous absorption threshold.

legend.orbit_circle: Circular orbit at radius rₙ = n²a₀; thicker line = higher probability of electron presence
legend.electron: Blue dot orbiting the nucleus at velocity vₙ
legend.proton_nucleus: Red dot at center representing the hydrogen nucleus (single proton)
legend.photon_arrow: Colored wavy arrow representing emitted/absorbed photon; color matches wavelength
legend.energy_level_line: Horizontal line in energy diagram at Eₙ = -13.6/n² eV; spacing decreases for higher n

concept_relationships.relationship: Bohr's quantization L = nℏ is equivalent to fitting n complete de Broglie wavelengths in the orbit: 2πrₙ = nλ
Both involve photon energy E = hf; photoelectric effect frees electron from metal, Bohr transitions involve photon absorption/emission within atom
Hydrogen ionization energy (13.6 eV) is tiny compared to nuclear binding energies (~MeV), showing atomic vs nuclear energy scales
Different spectral series (Lyman=UV, Balmer=visible, Paschen=IR) map to different regions of the electromagnetic spectrum
